docs.title: Welcome to OLLMCPC
docs.text:
OLLMCPC is a high-performance, terminal-based client for the Model Context Protocol (MCP). It acts as a bridge between powerful Large Language Models (LLMs) and your local system environment.

High Performance

Written in modern C++17, OLLMCPC is designed for speed and minimal resource usage, making it ideal for developers who want a snappy local AI assistant.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Multi-Provider Integration: Support for Ollama (local) and Gemini (cloud).
  • Built-in OS Assistant: A dedicated MCP server that provides tools for filesystem access, process management, and more.
  • Human-in-the-Loop (HIL): Built-in safety mechanism that prompts for user approval before executing system tools.
  • Extensible: Easily connect external MCP servers written in any language (Node.js, Python, Go).
  • Modern TUI: A beautiful terminal interface with colorized output and interactive menus.

docs.title: How it Works
docs.text:
OLLMCPC connects to an LLM provider and one or more MCP servers. When you ask a question, the LLM determines if it needs to use a tool provided by an MCP server. If so, OLLMCPC handles the communication, enforces safety checks, and returns the results to the LLM.

graph LR
    User -->|Prompts| Client[OLLMCPC Client]
    Client <-->|API| LLM[Ollama / Gemini]
    Client <-->|stdio| MCP[MCP Servers]
    MCP -->|Executes| Tools[Local Tools]

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Ready to dive in? Check out the Installation guide to get OLLMCPC running on your system.

docs.title: System Architecture
docs.text: OLLMCPC is built with a modular approach, separating the UI, LLM logic, and protocol handling.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The system consists of three main layers:

  1. Application Layer: Handles the CLI, Terminal UI (TUI), and configuration management.
  2. LLM Layer: A set of provider strategies that translate general tool/chat requests into provider-specific API calls (Ollama, Gemini).
  3. MCP Layer: Implements the Model Context Protocol. It manages server lifecycles, tool discovery, and execution.

docs.title: Flow Diagram
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant App as Client (App)
    participant LLM as LLM Provider
    participant MCP as MCP Registry
    participant Srv as MCP Server

    User->>App: Submits prompt
    App->>MCP: Get available tools
    MCP-->>App: Tool list
    App->>LLM: Send message + Tool schemas
    LLM->>App: Request tool execution
    App->>User: Request HIL Approval?
    User->>App: Approved!
    App->>MCP: Call tool(params)
    MCP->>Srv: Run tool
    Srv-->>MCP: Tool output
    MCP-->>App: Result
    App->>LLM: Return result to LLM
    LLM-->>App: Final response
    App->>User: Display response

docs.title: The Client (App)
docs.text: The main entry point (main.cpp) handles routing. The interactive loop (interactive.cpp) manages the state of the chat, rendering colors, and processing slash commands.

docs.title: The MCP Registry
docs.text: The MCPClient class is the central nervous system. It: * Launches local and external servers. * Aggregates tools from all connected servers into a single registry. * Dispatches tool calls to the correct server based on the server prefix.

docs.title: Server Proxies
docs.text: External servers (like those run via npx) are managed by MCPServerProxy. It handles: * Process spawning via pipe. * JSON-RPC communication over stdin/stdout. * Error handling and logging.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: OLLMCPC configuration is stored in a JSON file at ~/.ollmcpc.json.

docs.title: Configuration Wizard
docs.text:
You can initialize or update your configuration interactively:

./build/ollmcpc config

This will prompt you for: * Default LLM provider. * Ollama model name. * Gemini API Key and model name. * Human-in-the-Loop (HIL) toggle.

docs.title: Manual Configuration
docs.text:
Heres an example of a complete ~/.ollmcpc.json file:

{
  "default_provider": "ollama",
  "ollama_model": "functiongemma",
  "gemini_api_key": "YOUR_API_KEY_HERE",
  "gemini_model": "gemini-1.5-flash",
  "human_in_loop": true,
  "servers": [
    {
      "name": "filesystem",
      "command": ["npx", "-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/home/user/documents"],
      "enabled": true
    },
    {
      "name": "web-browsing",
      "command": ["npx", "-y", "mcp-fetch-server"],
      "enabled": true
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Options Explained
docs.text: Key Type Description default_provider string Either ollama, gemini, or manual. ollama_model string The model name in your local Ollama library. gemini_api_key string Your Google AI Studio API key. gemini_model string The specific Gemini model to use (e.g., gemini-1.5-pro). human_in_loop boolean If true, asks for permission before executing tools. servers array A list of external MCP servers to launch.

docs.title: Server Configuration
docs.text: Each object in the servers array requires: * name: A unique identifier for the server. * command: An array of strings representing the command to run (e.g., ["npx", "mcp-server-name"]). * enabled: (Optional) If set to false, the server won't be started automatically.

docs.title: GitHub Pages Deployment
docs.text: OLLMCPC documentation is automatically deployed to GitHub Pages using GitHub Actions.

docs.title: Setup Instructions
docs.text:
1. Enable GitHub Pages in your repository settings:
  2. Go to Settings → Pages
  3. Under Source, select Deploy from a branch
  4. Select the gh-pages branch

  5. Click Save

  6. Push to trigger deployment: 

    git add .
git commit -m "Add documentation"
git push origin main

  7. Access your docs: Your documentation will be available at: https://mohammed-alaa40123.github.io/ollmcpc_v2/

docs.title: How it Works
docs.text: The GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/docs.yml) automatically: 1. Triggers on every push to main or master branch 2. Sets up Python and installs MkDocs Material 3. Builds the documentation 4. Deploys to the gh-pages branch

docs.title: Manual Deployment
docs.text:
You can also deploy manually from your local machine:

# Install dependencies first
pip install mkdocs-material

# Deploy
mkdocs gh-deploy

This will build the docs and push them to the gh-pages branch.

docs.title: Custom Domain (Optional)
docs.text: To use a custom domain: 1. Add a CNAME file to the docs/ directory with your domain name 2. Configure your DNS provider to point to GitHub Pages 3. Enable custom domain in repository settings

docs.title: Installation Guide
docs.text: Follow these steps to set up OLLMCPC on your local machine.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: OLLMCPC requires a Linux environment (Ubuntu or Debian recommended).

docs.title: System Packages
docs.text:
Install the necessary build tools and libraries:

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y \
    build-essential \
    cmake \
    libcurl4-openssl-dev \
    libssl-dev \
    curl \
    nodejs \
    npm

docs.title: LLM Backends
docs.text:
• Ollama (Recommended for local LLM): Install via their official script: 
    curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
  • Gemini: No local installation required, but you will need an API Key from the Google AI Studio.

docs.title: Build from Source
docs.text:
Clone the repository and build using CMake:

git clone https://github.com/mohammed-alaa40123/ollmcpc_v2.git
cd ollmcpc_v2
mkdir build && cd build
cmake ..
make -j$(nproc)

This generates two main binaries in the build/ directory: 1. ollmcpc: The interactive client application. 2. mcp_server: The internal provider of system tools.

docs.title: Automated Setup
docs.text:
For a faster workflow, you can use the provided setup script:

chmod +x scripts/setup.sh
./scripts/setup.sh

The script will: 1. Check and install missing system dependencies. 2. Install Ollama if not present. 3. Build the project. 4. Pull the recommended models (functiongemma and qwen3:0.6b). 5. Create a default configuration file. 6. Install the binaries to /usr/local/bin.

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: Get up and running with OLLMCPC in 60 seconds.

docs.title: 1. Run the Setup Script
docs.text:
This handles dependencies and builds the project. 

chmod +x scripts/setup.sh
./scripts/setup.sh

docs.title: 2. Configure Your Models
docs.text:
Run the wizard to set your preferred provider. 

./build/ollmcpc config

docs.title: 3. Pull a Model (If using Ollama)
docs.text: ollama pull functiongemma

docs.title: 4. Start Chatting!
docs.text: ./build/ollmcpc serve

docs.title: 5. Try a Tool
docs.text:
Ask something like:

"What is my current CPU usage and what are the top 5 largest files in my home directory?"

Accept the HIL prompts and watch your AI interact with your system!

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Common issues and how to solve them.

docs.title: Missing libcurl
docs.text:
If cmake fails with a "Could not find CURL" error: 

sudo apt-get install libcurl4-openssl-dev

docs.title: Compiler too old
docs.text:
OLLMCPC requires C++17. If your compiler is too old: 

sudo apt-get install gcc-11 g++-11
Then run cmake with: CXX=g++-11 cmake ..

docs.title: Ollama Connection Refused
docs.text:
Ensure Ollama is running: 

ollama serve

docs.title: Server Error: "npx not found"
docs.text:
External servers often require Node.js. Ensure it's installed and in your PATH: 

node -v
npm -v

docs.title: Tool Execution Fails
docs.text:
Check debug.log in the root directory. It contains all JSON-RPC traffic between OLLMCPC and the MCP servers.

tail -f debug.log

docs.title: "Method not found"
docs.text: This usually means the MCP server doesn't support a specific feature. Ensure you are using a compatible version of the server.

docs.title: HIL Prompt not appearing
docs.text: Check your configuration (~/.ollmcpc.json) and ensure "human_in_loop": true is set. Note that "Manual Mode" bypasses HIL prompts as you are already manually selecting tools.

docs.title: Commands & Toggling
docs.text: OLLMCPC provides several internal commands to manage your session in real-time.

docs.title: Slash Commands
docs.text: In the interactive chat, any line starting with / is treated as a command rather than a prompt for the LLM.

docs.title: /help
docs.text: Displays a list of all available slash commands and their descriptions.

docs.title: /servers
docs.text: Lists all configured MCP servers and their current status: * Active: The server is running and communicating. * Disabled: The server is configured but turned off by the user. * Error: The server failed to start or lost connection.

docs.title: /toggle <name>
docs.text: Enables or disables a specific server by its name. * Example: /toggle filesystem * Effect: If the server was active, it will be shut down. If it was disabled, it will be launched and initialized.

docs.title: /exit
docs.text: Safely shuts down all MCP servers and exits the application.

docs.title: Human-in-the-Loop (HIL)
docs.text: Safety is a core principle of OLLMCPC. When HIL is enabled (default), the client will intercept every tool execution request.

docs.title: Approval Prompt
docs.text:
When the LLM wants to run a tool, you will see a colored box showing: * The tool name. * The arguments the LLM wants to pass.

You can then: * Press Enter: Approve the execution. * Type 'no': Deny the execution (the LLM will receive a "User denied execution" error). * Type 'edit': (Planned) Modify the arguments before execution.

docs.title: Custom MCP Servers
docs.text: You can easily extend OLLMCPC by adding your own MCP servers.

docs.title: Popular MCP Servers
docs.text: The Model Context Protocol has a growing ecosystem. You can find many servers on GitHub or via NPM.

docs.title: Examples:
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL: npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres postgres://...
  • Google Maps: npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-google-maps
  • GitHub: npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-github

docs.title: Adding to OLLMCPC
docs.text:
To add a new server, edit your ~/.ollmcpc.json and add a new entry to the servers array.

{
  "name": "my-sql-server",
  "command": ["npx", "-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres", "postgresql://localhost/mydb"],
  "enabled": true
}

docs.title: Creating Your Own Server
docs.text:
If you want to create a tool specifically for your workflow:

  1. Choose a Language: Node.js, Python, and Go have excellent MCP SDKs.
  2. Implement JSON-RPC: Your server must speak JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdin/stdout.
  3. Define Tools: Expose the tools your LLM needs.
  4. Register it: Add it to OLLMCPC configuration.

docs.title: Tool Naming
docs.text: OLLMCPC automatically prefixes your tools with the server name to avoid collisions. * Server: filesystem * Tool: read_file * LLM sees: filesystem_read_file

docs.title: Application Layer
docs.text: The Application layer manages the user interaction and high-level orchestration of the system.

docs.title: Entry Point (main.cpp)
docs.text: Located in src/main.cpp. It uses a custom CLI parser to route commands: * serve: Starts the interactive chat session. * config: Starts the configuration wizard. * list: Lists available models from the configured providers.

docs.title: Interactive Mode (interactive.cpp)
docs.text: The "Brain" of the UI, found in src/app/interactive.cpp.

docs.title: Responsibilities:
docs.text:
• Chat Loop: Keeps the conversation going until the user exits.
  • Slash Commands: Processes commands like /help, /servers, and /toggle.
  • HIL Logic: Implements the Human-in-the-Loop check before any tool is executed.
  • Terminal Styling: Uses utils/terminal.h to provide a rich, colorized output.

docs.title: Configuration Manager (config.cpp)
docs.text: Found in src/app/config.cpp.

docs.title: Features:
docs.text:
• JSON Persistence: Saves and loads settings from ~/.ollmcpc.json.
  • Interactive Setup: Provides a terminal-based UI for initial configuration.
  • Default Injection: Ensures healthy defaults are present if the config file is missing or corrupted.

docs.title: Key Files:
docs.text:
• src/include/app/interactive.h: UI definitions.
  • src/include/app/config.h: Configuration structures.
  • src/src/app/interactive.cpp: Implementation of the UI logic.
  • src/src/app/config.cpp: Implementation of persistence logic.

docs.title: LLM Providers
docs.text: OLLMCPC uses a Strategy Pattern to support multiple LLM backends. All providers inherit from the LLMProvider base class.

docs.title: Base Class (llm_provider.h)
docs.text: Defines the interface for communication: * chat(messages, tools): Sends a prompt and available tools to the LLM. * list_models(): Returns a list of supported models.

docs.title: Ollama Provider (ollama_provider.cpp)
docs.text: Connects to a local Ollama instance (usually at http://localhost:11434).

docs.title: Features:
docs.text:
• Standard chat-completion API.
  • Native tool support (functions).
  • Streaming responses.

docs.title: Gemini Provider (gemini_provider.cpp)
docs.text: Connects to Google's Gemini API.

docs.title: Features:
docs.text:
• High latency but powerful reasoning.
  • Strict Tool-Calling schemas.
  • Requires an API Key.

docs.title: Manual Provider (manual_provider.cpp)
docs.text: A special "debug" provider that doesn't use an LLM.

docs.title: Purpose:
docs.text:
• Allows the user to manually select tools from a menu.
  • Perfect for testing if a new MCP tool is working correctly without spending tokens or waiting for local inference.

docs.title: Key Files:
docs.text:
• src/include/llm/llm_provider.h: Abstract base class.
  • src/src/llm/ollama_provider.cpp: Ollama implementation.
  • src/src/llm/gemini_provider.cpp: Gemini implementation.
  • src/src/llm/manual_provider.cpp: Interactive tool selector.

docs.title: MCP Backend
docs.text: The MCP Backend is the core of OLLMCPC's power, implementing the Model Context Protocol communication.

docs.title: MCP Client (client.cpp)
docs.text: The MCPClient manages the lifecycle of all connected servers.

docs.title: Functions:
docs.text:
• Initialization: Sends the initialize request to all servers.
  • Tool Aggregation: Merges tools from all servers into a single list for the LLM.
  • Routing: When a tool called filesystem_read_file is requested, it knows to strip the filesystem_ prefix and send the read_file request to the correct server.

docs.title: Server Proxy (server_proxy.cpp)
docs.text: Handles the low-level details of running an external MCP server.

docs.title: Implementation:
docs.text:
• Pipe Communication: Uses dup2 and pipe to capture stdin and stdout of the child process.
  • JSON-RPC 2.0: Implements the standard JSON-RPC protocol over the pipes.
  • Async Reading: Uses threads to read output from the server so the main client stays responsive.

docs.title: Internal Server (mcp_server target)
docs.text: A standalone binary built from src/mcp/server.cpp. It provides the "OS Assistant" tools.

docs.title: Key Tools:
docs.text:
• execute_shell: Run arbitrary commands safely.
  • cpu_usage: Monitor system load.
  • process_list: See what's running.

docs.title: Key Files:
docs.text:
• src/include/mcp/client.h: The MCP registry and manager.
  • src/include/mcp/server_proxy.h: External process bridge.
  • src/src/mcp/client.cpp: Logic for cross-server coordination.
  • src/src/mcp/server_proxy.cpp: Logic for stdio/pipe communication.

docs.title: Utilities
docs.text: OLLMCPC includes a set of lightweight utilities to minimize external dependencies.

docs.title: CURL Wrapper (curl_utils.cpp)
docs.text: A thin, thread-safe wrapper around libcurl for making HTTP requests to Ollama and Gemini APIs.

docs.title: Terminal Styling (terminal.h)
docs.text: Provides macros and functions for: * ANSI color output. * Box drawing. * Progress indicators (simplified). * Bold/Italic/Underline text.

docs.title: JSON Handling (json_utils.h)
docs.text: While the project uses nlohmann/json for complex parsing, the utils folder contains helper functions for: * Safe value extraction. * Pretty-printing JSON logic specifically for the terminal.

docs.title: Logging (logger.h)
docs.text: A simple logger that outputs to debug.log. It can be configured for different levels: * DEBUG: Protocol messages, raw JSON-RPC. * INFO: Normal operation. * ERROR: Connection failures or protocol violations.

docs.title: Key Files:
docs.text:
• src/include/utils/curl_utils.h: Network communication.
  • src/include/utils/terminal.h: Aesthetic output tools.
  • src/include/utils/logger.h: Debugging and monitoring.
